title: TCP Flags
description: 9 control bits in the TCP header that signal connection state and control data flow.
fullExplanation: TCP flags are 1-bit fields in the TCP header that control the state of a connection. Each flag has a specific meaning and is used in different phases of the TCP lifecycle. Nmap and other tools craft packets with specific flag combinations to probe firewalls and detect OS fingerprints. Unusual flag combinations (NULL, FIN, Xmas) are used in stealth scans.

flags.description: Synchronize — initiates connection. Used in 3-way handshake (step 1 and 2).
Acknowledgement — confirms receipt of data. Set in all packets after initial SYN.
Finish — gracefully closes connection. Initiates 4-way teardown.
Reset — abruptly terminates connection. Sent on error or to reject connection.
Push — tells receiver to pass data to application immediately, don't buffer.
Urgent — urgent pointer field is valid. Rarely used in modern protocols.
ECN Echo — signals congestion detected (used with CWR for ECN).
Congestion Window Reduced — sender reduced congestion window in response to ECE.
Nonce Sum — experimental ECN nonce (RFC 3540). Rarely used.

commonCombinations.meaning: Abrupt connection reset — port closed or error
Data segment — push to application immediately
No flags — used in Nmap NULL scan to probe firewalls
Xmas scan — all 'lit up' flags, used in Nmap Xmas scan

keyFacts: RST vs FIN: RST abruptly kills connection, FIN gracefully closes it
RST sent when: port is closed, connection is invalid, or firewall rejects
SYN flood: send thousands of SYNs without completing handshake
TCP RST attack: inject RST packet to kill existing connection
Nmap NULL scan: no flags — RFC says closed port sends RST, open port ignores
Nmap Xmas scan: FIN+PSH+URG — same behavior as NULL scan
ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification): ECE+CWR flags signal congestion without packet loss
